explanation: RIP, OSPF and IS-IS all run inside a single autonomous system, which is what makes a protocol an Interior Gateway Protocol, so the answer is 'All of them'. The trap is picking one of them out of habit: the odd one out would have been BGP, the exterior gateway protocol used between autonomous systems, and it is not on the list.

Reading 'best' as best case, the search key is compared against the root on the first probe and matches, so the work is constant: theta(1). Note the stem is sloppy: if it instead meant a BST built by inserting already-sorted keys, the tree degenerates into a linked list and search costs theta(n), so know both readings. The average/typical BST search is theta(log n), which is the distractor most people jump to.

The Agile Manifesto's four values are: individuals and interactions over processes and tools; working software over comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; responding to change over following a plan. Option A states 'contract negotiation over customer collaboration', which is the third value reversed, so it is the one NOT in the manifesto. B, C and D are the other three values quoted (loosely) as written.

Answer unverified: this item is quarantined as unanswerable. The K-map figure is missing, and the stem as transcribed makes several options simultaneously true: a circled group whose product keeps all four literals (ABCD) is a single cell, so it is literally a minterm (A); if that cell has no adjacent 1 to merge with, it is also a prime implicant (C) and the only cover of its own minterm, hence an essential prime implicant (D). Without the map you cannot check adjacency, so the intended key cannot be recovered. For revision, learn the definitions: implicant = any product term covering only 1s; prime implicant = an implicant that cannot be enlarged by dropping a literal; essential prime implicant = a prime implicant that is the sole cover of some 1.

The Data Link layer is the one whose PDU is the frame, and it operates hop-by-hop over a single physical link, adding an FCS/CRC trailer for error detection plus optional retransmission. Transport is the classic trap: it is also 'reliable', but end-to-end between hosts and its PDU is a segment, not a frame. Physical carries raw bits, and Network carries packets and does routing, neither works in frames.

Answer unverified: the item ships with no marked key (no option is flagged correct in the source). Mechanically the answer is Class A: classful addressing keys off the first octet, and 100 falls in the range 1-126 (leading bit 0), which is Class A. Class B is 128-191, Class C is 192-223 and Class D (multicast) is 224-239. The '.1.3.0' part is irrelevant; only the first octet decides the class.

For i = 0 through 4 the 'continue' fires and jumps straight to i++, so nothing prints. For i = 5, 6, 7 both ifs are false and cout runs, printing '5, 6, 7, '. At i = 8 the 'break' executes before the cout is reached, so 8 is never printed and the loop ends. Option C (5,6,7,8,9) is the trap: 'break' exits the loop rather than skipping just that iteration, and it sits above the cout.

Answer unverified: this item is quarantined as unanswerable. The mux figure is missing, so the data-pin order and which of C/D is the select MSB cannot be confirmed. Taking the wiring exactly as transcribed (I0 = A XOR B, I1 = B, I2 = I3 = B', selects C and D), the output works out to F = B'C + A'BC' + AB'D' + BC'D, which is not any named operation (not an adder, comparator or majority gate), and swapping the select order gives a different function. Method to remember: a 4x1 mux output is F = S1'S0'I0 + S1'S0*I1 + S1*S0'I2 + S1*S0*I3, so you substitute each data input and simplify.

A deadlock is a set of processes each blocked holding one resource and waiting on a resource held by another process in the set, so none of them can ever proceed. The key word is 'forever': the processes are alive but permanently blocked, not terminated (A) and not destroying each other (B). Remember the four Coffman conditions that must all hold: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait.

Propagation delay is the length of the longest path from an input to the output (the critical path), not the total number of gates. The circuit has 5 gates arranged in 3 levels (inverters feeding two middle gates feeding one output gate), so a signal passes through 3 gates: 3 x 1T = 3T. Option C (5T) is the trap: it sums all 5 gates, which would only be right if they were all in series.

RISC is defined by a small set of simple, fixed-length instructions that mostly execute in one cycle, which is what makes deep pipelining and hardwired control possible. Options B and C describe CISC: a large instruction set with instructions that can operate directly on memory operands. In RISC only load and store touch memory (load/store architecture); everything else works on registers.

Multimedia networking is conventionally split into exactly three classes: streaming stored audio/video, streaming live audio/video, and real-time interactive (voice/video over IP). 'Sensitive streaming' is not one of them, it is an invented term, so it is the odd one out. Do not confuse it with the property that multimedia traffic is delay-sensitive but loss-tolerant, which is probably where the distractor's wording came from.

Two's complement = invert every bit, then add 1. Invert 1111 1111 1111 -> 0000 0000 0000, then add 1 -> 0000 0000 0001. Sanity check with values: 1111 1111 1111 is -1 in 12-bit two's complement, and negating -1 gives +1, which is exactly 0000 0000 0001. The classic slip is stopping at the one's complement (all zeros) and forgetting the +1.

Answer unverified: this item is quarantined as unanswerable. The truth table the stem refers to is missing, and SR and JK differ in exactly one row, which is precisely the information the table carried: for inputs 11, SR is invalid/forbidden while JK toggles (Qnext = Q'). So the distinguishing check is: look at the 1-1 row, if it toggles it is JK, if it is marked X/invalid it is SR.

An ARP request is broadcast on the LAN, so the Ethernet destination MAC is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF, which is all 1's, and that is the intended answer. The stem is loose: inside the ARP payload the target hardware address field is filled with all 0's precisely because it is the unknown being asked for. Rule of thumb: the frame's destination is all 1's (broadcast), the unknown field being queried is all 0's.

A node is the single element of a linked list: it stores a data field plus a pointer (link) to the next node, and in a doubly linked list also a pointer to the previous node. The chain of next-pointers starting from the head is what holds the list together, which is why a list has no random indexing and must be traversed from the head, giving O(n) access but O(1) insert/delete once you hold the node.

The difference is only in the output function: a Moore machine's output depends on the present state alone, while a Mealy machine's output depends on the present state AND the present input. Consequences worth quoting: Mealy usually needs fewer states and reacts in the same cycle as the input, but its outputs can glitch with the inputs; Moore's outputs are stable/registered but appear one clock later. The next-state logic is the same for both (state and input).

HTTP uses well-known TCP port 80. Pair it with HTTPS on 443, which is the distractor people reach for; other common ones on the same exam sheet are FTP 20/21, SSH 22, Telnet 23, SMTP 25, DNS 53 and DHCP 67/68.

TLB = Translation Lookaside Buffer, a small fully/highly associative cache inside the MMU that holds recently used virtual-page-to-physical-frame translations. On a TLB hit the physical address is formed without touching memory; on a miss the hardware or OS walks the page table in memory (slow) and then loads the entry into the TLB. It caches address translations, not data, which is what separates it from the L1/L2 data cache.

Answer unverified: the item ships with no key and is open-ended. The standard taxonomy is: binary search trees (plain BST, plus the self-balancing ones, AVL, red-black, splay, treap) and m-way / multi-way search trees (2-3 tree, B-tree, B+ tree used for database and filesystem indexes), with ternary search trees as a specialized string variant. The organizing property common to all of them is the ordering invariant: keys in the left subtree < node key < keys in the right subtree, generalized to m-1 keys and m children in the multi-way case.
